_meta.title: Researched skills for Nuclexa
_meta.description: Real, individually-verified Agent Skills (SKILL.md packages) discovered via research, NOT yet added to the site. Each entry was confirmed by fetching the actual SKILL.md from its source repo. Deduped against the existing catalog. Labels: `author` (source/org, e.g. 'Anthropic') and `category` (one of the 12 landing-page categories, e.g. 'design'). Shape mirrors scripts/skills-data.json (name/description/trigger_phrases/tags/source_url/markdown_instructions) plus the label fields (author, category, model, displayName, verified).

label_legend.author: Origin / publisher. Used as the 'Anthropic'-style label.
label_legend.category: One of: development, security, devops, research, design, writing, data-science, productivity, education, browser-automation, integrations, marketing.
label_legend.model: Target AI ecosystem: anthropic | openai | google | meta | mistral | universal.

skills.name: pdf
skills.description: Reads, extracts, creates, and manipulates PDF files — text/table extraction, merging, splitting, rotating, watermarking, form-filling, encryption, and OCR. Use whenever a task involves reading from or producing .pdf files.
skills.author: Anthropic
skills.markdown_instructions:
# PDF Processing

Handle reading, extracting, creating, and editing PDF documents.

## Tool selection
- **pypdf** — core manipulation: merge multiple PDFs, split into individual pages, rotate pages in 90 degree increments, add watermarks, encrypt/password-protect, read and modify metadata, extract embedded images.
- **pdfplumber** — extract text while preserving layout and pull tabular data; tables can be converted directly into pandas DataFrames for analysis.
- **reportlab** — build PDFs programmatically, from a simple single page to complex multi-page formatted reports.
- **pytesseract** — OCR for scanned/image-only PDFs.
- **qpdf**, **pdftotext**, **pdftk** — command-line tools for batch operations and form-filling.

## Workflow
1. Identify whether the task is extraction, manipulation, or creation.
2. For extraction, prefer pdfplumber to keep layout/tables intact.
3. For manipulation (merge/split/rotate/encrypt), use pypdf.
4. For new documents, build with reportlab.
5. For scanned input, run pytesseract OCR first, then process the text.

## Important note
Never use Unicode subscript/superscript characters when generating PDFs — the built-in fonts lack those glyphs and they render as solid black boxes. Instead use ReportLab's XML markup tags such as `<sub>` and `<super>`. See REFERENCE.md for advanced usage and FORMS.md for form-filling.
skills.verified: Fetched raw SKILL.md from anthropics/skills/skills/pdf

skills.name: pptx
skills.description: Creates, edits, and extracts content from PowerPoint (.pptx) presentations with strong visual-design guidance and a render-and-inspect QA loop. Use whenever a request mentions decks, slides, or presentations.
skills.author: Anthropic
skills.markdown_instructions:
# PPTX Builder

Handle all PowerPoint operations: creating decks, editing existing files, extracting content, and combining slides. Trigger on mentions of "deck," "slides," "presentation," or `.pptx` files.

## Workflows
- **Read content:** run `python -m markitdown presentation.pptx` for text extraction, or use `thumbnail.py` for a visual overview.
- **Edit existing decks:** unpack the file, modify slides, then repack (see `editing.md`).
- **Create from scratch:** use PptxGenJS when no template exists (see `pptxgenjs.md`).

## Design principles
Avoid generic white backgrounds with plain bullet points. Instead:
- Choose a topic-specific color scheme where one color dominates (60-70% of visual weight) with 1-2 supporting tones.
- Put a visual element on every slide — image, chart, icon, or shape.
- Vary layouts across slides rather than repeating an identical structure.
- Use 36-44pt bold titles and consistent 0.3-0.5" spacing.
- Never underline titles with accent lines (a hallmark of AI-generated slides); use whitespace or background color instead.

## Quality assurance
Always assume problems exist. Run content QA with markitdown, convert slides to images, and use subagents for a fresh-eyes visual inspection. Check for overlapping elements, text overflow, low contrast, and leftover placeholders. Complete at least one fix-and-verify cycle before declaring the deck done.

Dependencies: markitdown[pptx], Pillow, pptxgenjs (npm), LibreOffice (soffice), Poppler (pdftoppm).
skills.verified: Fetched raw SKILL.md from anthropics/skills/skills/pptx

skills.name: docx
skills.description: Creates, reads, and edits Word (.docx) documents with professional formatting — tables, images, headers/footers, tracked changes, comments, and TOCs. NOTE: overlaps with the existing 'docx-creation' catalog entry, but this is Anthropic's official document skill with deeper OOXML editing internals.
skills.author: Anthropic
skills.markdown_instructions:
# DOCX Builder

Create, read, and edit Word (.docx) documents. Use for any Word-document request — not for PDFs, spreadsheets, or Google Docs.

## Capabilities
- **Create:** use the `docx` npm package (JavaScript) to generate files with tables, images, headers/footers, and formatted text.
- **Read:** extract text via pandoc, or unpack the XML for raw access.
- **Edit:** unpack -> modify XML -> repack.

## Editing workflow
1. Unpack: `python scripts/office/unpack.py document.docx unpacked/`
2. Edit XML files in `unpacked/word/` directly.
3. Repack: `python scripts/office/pack.py unpacked/ output.docx --original document.docx`
For comments, use `comment.py` with pre-escaped XML text before adding markers to document.xml.

## Critical rules
- Always set page size explicitly (docx defaults to A4, not US Letter).
- Tables need both a `columnWidths` array AND per-cell `width` in DXA units.
- Use `LevelFormat.BULLET` with numbering config for lists; never insert unicode bullet characters.
- Images require a `type` parameter (png/jpg/gif/bmp/svg).
- Tracked changes: mark insertions with `<w:ins>`, deletions with `<w:del>` using `<w:delText>`.

## Measurements
1440 DXA = 1 inch. US Letter = 12,240 x 15,840 DXA; 1-inch margins leave 9,360 DXA of content width.
skills.verified: Fetched raw SKILL.md from anthropics/skills/skills/docx

skills.name: xlsx
skills.description: Creates, edits, and analyzes spreadsheets (.xlsx/.xlsm/.csv/.tsv) with formula-first calculations and financial-model formatting conventions. NOTE: overlaps with the existing 'xlsx-analysis' catalog entry, but this is Anthropic's official document skill.
skills.author: Anthropic
skills.markdown_instructions:
# XLSX Spreadsheet Builder

Create, edit, and analyze .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, and .tsv files. Trigger when spreadsheets are the primary input or output (opening files, adding columns, computing formulas, cleaning data, converting tabular formats).

## Professional standards
- Use consistent professional fonts (Arial, Times New Roman).
- Deliver files with zero formula errors.
- Preserve existing template formats when updating.

## Financial-model color coding
- Blue: user-changeable inputs
- Black: formulas and calculations
- Green: internal workbook links
- Red: external file references
- Yellow background: key assumptions

## Number formatting
- Years as text ("2024"), currency with units in headers ("$mm"), zeros displayed as "-", percentages at 0.0%, negatives in parentheses (123).

## Critical rule
Always use Excel formulas instead of computing values in Python and hardcoding them, so the spreadsheet stays dynamic and updateable.

## Workflow
1. Choose the tool (pandas for analysis, openpyxl for formulas/formatting).
2. Create or load the file. 3. Modify data, formulas, and formatting. 4. Save.
5. Recalculate formulas with `scripts/recalc.py` (requires LibreOffice). 6. Verify zero errors via the recalc script's JSON output and fix any issues.
skills.verified: Fetched raw SKILL.md from anthropics/skills/skills/xlsx

skills.name: canvas-design
skills.description: Creates original, museum-quality visual art and designs as PNG and PDF by first articulating a design philosophy, then expressing it on the canvas. Use for posters, art pieces, and static visual designs.
skills.author: Anthropic
skills.markdown_instructions:
# Canvas Design

Create sophisticated, original visual art in PDF and PNG formats. Never copy existing artists' work.

## Two-step process
1. **Design philosophy** — articulate an aesthetic movement as a short manifesto before drawing anything.
2. **Canvas expression** — visually manifest that philosophy through form, color, and composition.

## Core principles
- **Visual-first:** ideas communicate through space, form, color, and composition — not paragraphs. Keep text minimal and integrated as a design element, never as explanation.
- **Craftsmanship:** work should appear meticulously labored, the product of countless hours by someone at the top of their field.
- **Conceptual depth:** embed subtle thematic references — sophisticated enough that those familiar with the subject recognize the concept intuitively, while others experience abstract artistic mastery.
- **Restraint:** during refinement, make the existing composition more cohesive rather than adding elements.

## Output requirements
- Single-page designs unless multi-page is requested.
- Museum/magazine-quality execution.
- Minimal, strategic typography from professional font libraries.
- Flawless spacing with proper margins and no overlapping elements.
skills.verified: Fetched raw SKILL.md from anthropics/skills/skills/canvas-design

skills.name: brand-guidelines
skills.description: Applies a consistent corporate visual identity — color palette, typography, and styling — to artifacts as a post-processing step. Use when work needs brand-consistent visual formatting.
skills.author: Anthropic
skills.markdown_instructions:
# Brand Guidelines

Apply an official visual identity to artifacts. Use when a project needs branding, corporate/visual identity, post-processing, styling, brand colors, typography, or consistent visual formatting.

## Color palette
- Primary tones: a dark (e.g. `#141413`) and a light (e.g. `#faf9f5`).
- Accent options (e.g. orange `#d97757`, plus blue and green) for visual variety while staying on-brand.
- Apply colors via precise RGB specifications for consistency across displays. Cycle through accent colors for non-text shapes while preserving visual hierarchy.

## Typography
- Apply a display family to headings (24pt and larger), a serif/body family for body text.
- Fall back automatically to system fonts (Arial headings, Georgia body) when brand fonts are unavailable.
- Select fonts intelligently based on element type and size to ensure readability across platforms.

## Application notes
The styling system works without mandatory font installation by using system-installed fonts when available, though pre-installing the brand fonts yields optimal results. Maintain proper contrast and readability throughout.
skills.verified: Fetched raw SKILL.md from anthropics/skills/skills/brand-guidelines

skills.name: theme-factory
skills.description: Styles any artifact (slides, docs, reports, HTML landing pages) with a cohesive font + color theme, offering 10 preset themes or generating a custom one on the fly.
skills.author: Anthropic
trigger_phrases: style my slides with a theme
pick a theme for this artifact
skills.markdown_instructions:
# Theme Factory

Style artifacts — slides, docs, reports, HTML landing pages — with a consistent theme. Provides 10 preset themes (each a cohesive color palette with hex codes plus complementary typography) and can generate a new theme on the fly.

## Workflow
1. Display the theme showcase for visual reference.
2. Ask the user for their preference.
3. Get explicit confirmation of the chosen theme.
4. Apply the selected theme's colors and fonts consistently throughout the artifact.

## Custom themes
When no preset fits, have the user describe their needs, generate a new theme, present it for review, and apply it following the same confirm-then-apply process.

## Quality
Theme specifications live in a `themes/` directory. Always ensure proper contrast and readability while keeping every element visually consistent.

skills.name: web-artifacts-builder
skills.description: Builds elaborate, multi-component HTML artifacts with React, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, and shadcn/ui, then bundles everything into a single self-contained HTML file. Use for complex artifacts needing state, routing, or shadcn components.
skills.author: Anthropic
trigger_phrases: make a multi-page web app artifact
bundle this into one HTML file
skills.markdown_instructions:
# Web Artifacts Builder

Build elaborate, multi-component HTML artifacts using React 18 + TypeScript + Vite + Tailwind CSS + shadcn/ui, bundled with Parcel. Use for complex artifacts requiring state management, routing, or shadcn/ui components — not for simple single-file HTML/JSX artifacts.

## Steps
1. **Initialize:** `bash scripts/init-artifact.sh <project-name>` then `cd <project-name>`. This scaffolds React+TypeScript (Vite), Tailwind with shadcn theming, `@/` path aliases, 40+ pre-installed shadcn/ui components, all Radix UI deps, and Parcel bundling config.
2. **Develop:** edit the generated files to build the artifact.
3. **Bundle:** `bash scripts/bundle-artifact.sh` produces `bundle.html`, a self-contained file with all JS, CSS, and dependencies inlined.
4. **Share:** present `bundle.html` to the user as an artifact.
5. **Test (optional):** only if needed/requested, visualize with Playwright/Puppeteer. Avoid testing upfront — it adds latency.

## Design guidance
To avoid "AI slop," avoid excessive centered layouts, purple gradients, uniformly rounded corners, and the Inter font. Reference shadcn/ui components at https://ui.shadcn.com/docs/components.
skills.verified: Fetched raw SKILL.md from anthropics/skills/skills/web-artifacts-builder

skills.name: doc-coauthoring
skills.description: Guides collaborative creation of structured documents (proposals, specs, decision docs, documentation) through context gathering, section-by-section refinement, and fresh-reader testing.
skills.author: Anthropic
trigger_phrases: write a decision doc with me
skills.markdown_instructions:
# Document Co-Authoring

Guide structured collaborative document creation. Offer this workflow when users mention writing documentation, proposals, specs, decision docs, or similar structured content. Prioritize reader comprehension over authorial speed.

## Stage 1 — Context gathering
Ask meta-questions about document type, audience, desired impact, and format. Encourage information dumping and pull context directly from integrations (Slack, Google Drive, etc.) when available. Ask clarifying questions to identify gaps before drafting.

## Stage 2 — Refinement & structure
Build the document section by section: ask clarifying questions, brainstorm 5-20 options, let the user curate (keep/remove/combine), check for gaps, draft into an artifact or file, then refine iteratively. Prioritize the sections with the highest unknowns first.

## Stage 3 — Reader testing
Test whether the document works for fresh readers who lack the author's context. In Claude Code with sub-agents this runs automatically; otherwise the user tests in a fresh conversation by asking predicted reader questions and checking for ambiguities, false assumptions, and contradictions.
skills.verified: Fetched raw SKILL.md from anthropics/skills/skills/doc-coauthoring

skills.markdown_instructions:
# Internal Communications

Help write all kinds of internal company communications using standardized formats. Supports seven main types: 3P updates, company newsletters, FAQ responses, status reports, leadership updates, project updates, and incident reports.

## Process
1. Identify what type of communication is needed.
2. Locate the corresponding guideline file in the examples directory.
3. Follow the specific instructions in that file for the correct format and tone.

## Example templates
- `3P-updates.md` — Progress/Plans/Problems team communications.
- `Company-newsletter.md` — organization-wide messaging.
- `Faq-answers.md` — common-question responses.
- `General-comms.md` — fallback for other communication needs.

If a request doesn't match an existing guideline, ask the user for clarification about the desired format before drafting.

skills.name: slack-gif-creator
skills.description: Creates animated GIFs optimized for Slack (emoji and message sizes) using PIL primitives, with a GIFBuilder, validators, and easing utilities. Use when a user asks for an animated GIF for Slack.
skills.author: Anthropic
skills.markdown_instructions:
# Slack GIF Creator

Knowledge and utilities for creating animated GIFs optimized for Slack. Use when a user requests an animated GIF for Slack.

## Slack requirements
- Emoji GIFs: 128x128. Message GIFs: 480x480.
- FPS 10-30 (lower = smaller file). Colors 48-128 (fewer = smaller). Keep emoji GIFs under 3 seconds.

## Core workflow
1. Create a `GIFBuilder(width, height, fps)` from `core.gif_builder`.
2. Generate frames as PIL Images, drawing with `ImageDraw` primitives, and `builder.add_frame(frame)`.
3. Save with optimization: `builder.save('out.gif', num_colors=48, optimize_for_emoji=True, remove_duplicates=True)`.

## Drawing
Use PIL `ImageDraw` primitives (ellipse, polygon, line, rectangle). Always use `width>=2` outlines. Add depth with gradient backgrounds, layered shapes, highlights and glows, vibrant complementary colors, and strong contrast.

## Utilities
- `core.validators` — `validate_gif()` / `is_slack_ready()` to confirm Slack compliance.
- `core.easing` — `interpolate(start, end, t, easing=...)` with linear, ease_in/out, bounce_out, elastic_out, back_out.
- `core.frame_composer` — blank/gradient backgrounds and circle/text/star helpers.

## Animation concepts
Shake, pulse/heartbeat, bounce (bounce_out easing + gravity), spin (PIL rotate), fade (alpha blend), slide, zoom, and particle bursts. Combine concepts for richer animation.

Dependencies: `pip install pillow imageio numpy`.
skills.verified: Fetched raw SKILL.md from anthropics/skills/skills/slack-gif-creator

skills.name: claude-api
skills.description: A reference skill for building on the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model IDs, pricing, parameters, streaming, tool use, MCP, managed agents, prompt caching, token counting, and model migration.
skills.author: Anthropic
trigger_phrases: which Claude model should I use
how do I stream from the Claude API
set up tool use with Claude
migrate to a new Claude model
skills.markdown_instructions:
# Claude API Reference

Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK: model IDs, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, and migration.

## Default configuration
- **Model:** the latest Opus-tier model by default unless explicitly told otherwise. Other models trade off capability, latency, and pricing.
- **Thinking:** use adaptive thinking for moderately complex tasks.
- **Streaming:** default for long input/output or high `max_tokens`.
- **Output:** retrieve complete responses with `.get_final_message()` / `.finalMessage()`.

## Language detection
Identify the project language by scanning file extensions and config files, then read the language-specific docs at `{language}/claude-api/`.

## API surface
Everything routes through `POST /v1/messages`. Tools, structured outputs, and prompt caching are parameters of this single endpoint, not separate APIs.

## Common pitfalls
- Re-measure token counts when switching model families (tokenizers differ); use `count_tokens`.
- Don't truncate long inputs — discuss chunking or summarization with the user.
- Always check `stop_reason` before reading content.
- Never reimplement SDK helpers; use `stream.finalMessage()`, typed exceptions, and SDK-exported types.
skills.verified: Fetched raw SKILL.md from anthropics/skills/skills/claude-api

skills.name: prisma-orm-v7-upgrade
skills.description: Step-by-step migration guide from Prisma ORM v6 to v7, covering the new prisma-client generator, required driver adapters, prisma.config.ts, ESM-first setup, explicit env loading, and replacing Prisma.validator with satisfies.
skills.author: Prisma
skills.markdown_instructions:
# Upgrade to Prisma ORM 7

Complete guide for migrating from Prisma ORM v6 to v7. This upgrade introduces breaking changes around the new `prisma-client` generator, driver adapters, `prisma.config.ts`, explicit env loading, and generated client entrypoints.

## Breaking changes
- Generator provider: `prisma-client-js` -> `prisma-client` (default).
- Output path: now required and explicit.
- Driver adapters: now required for SQL providers.
- Config: `.env` + schema -> `prisma.config.ts`.
- Env loading: automatic -> manual (dotenv).
- Module format: ESM-first (`moduleFormat = "cjs"` supported).
- Type-safe fragments: `Prisma.validator()` -> TypeScript `satisfies`.

## Upgrade steps
1. `npm install @prisma/client@7` and `npm install -D prisma@7`.
2. Choose module format (ESM default).
3. Update the generator block to `provider = "prisma-client"` with explicit `output`.
4. Create `prisma.config.ts` importing `dotenv/config` and `defineConfig`/`env` from `prisma/config`.
5. Install a driver adapter (`@prisma/adapter-pg pg`, `@prisma/adapter-neon`, etc.).
6. Update client instantiation to pass `{ adapter }`.
7. Replace `Prisma.validator()` with `satisfies Prisma.UserSelect`.
8. Run `npx prisma generate`.

**Important:** MongoDB projects should stay on Prisma 6.x — there is no SQL adapter for Mongo in 7.x. Requires Node 20.19.0+ and TypeScript 5.4.0+.
skills.verified: Cloned github.com/prisma/skills; read prisma-upgrade-v7/SKILL.md (author: prisma, v7.6.0)

skills.name: prisma-client-api
skills.description: Comprehensive Prisma Client API reference covering model queries, CRUD, filters and operators, relations, transactions, raw SQL safety, and client construction with driver adapters.
skills.author: Prisma
skills.markdown_instructions:
# Prisma Client API Reference

Complete API reference for Prisma Client covering model queries, filtering, relations, and client methods.

## When to apply
Writing database queries, CRUD operations, filtering/sorting, relations, transactions, and client configuration.

## Rule categories (by priority)
1. Client construction — `PrismaClient` setup, adapter wiring, logging.
2. Model queries — CRUD and bulk operations.
3. Query shape — `select`, `include`, `omit`, sort, pagination.
4. Filters — scalar and logical filter operators.
5. Relations — relation reads and nested writes.
6. Transactions — array and interactive transaction patterns.
7. Raw SQL — `$queryRaw` / `$executeRaw` safety.
8. Client methods — lifecycle, extensions, and `satisfies` patterns.

## Client instantiation
```typescript
import { PrismaClient } from '../generated/client'
import { PrismaPg } from '@prisma/adapter-pg'
const adapter = new PrismaPg({ connectionString: process.env.DATABASE_URL })
const prisma = new PrismaClient({ adapter })
```
Detailed rules for each category live in `references/` and should be applied based on the query being written.
skills.verified: Cloned github.com/prisma/skills; read prisma-client-api/SKILL.md (author: prisma, v7.6.0)

skills.name: convex-migration-helper
skills.description: Plans and runs safe Convex schema and data migrations using the widen-migrate-narrow pattern and the @convex-dev/migrations component. Use for breaking schema changes, backfills, table reshaping, or zero-downtime rollouts.
skills.author: Convex
skills.markdown_instructions:
# Convex Migration Helper

Safely migrate Convex schemas and data when making breaking changes (adding required fields, changing field types, splitting/merging tables, renaming/deleting fields).

## Key concept
Convex will not let you deploy a schema that doesn't match the data at rest. So migrations follow: **widen the schema, migrate the data, narrow the schema.** Migrations run online — the app keeps serving while data updates asynchronously in batches, so code must handle both old and new formats during the window. Prefer new fields over changing types, and don't delete data — deprecate with `v.optional` plus a comment.

## Deployment workflow
- **Deploy 1 (Widen):** schema allows both formats; code reads both, writes the new format.
- **Between deploys (Migrate):** backfill existing documents; verify completion.
- **Deploy 2 (Narrow):** require the new format only; remove old-format handling.

## Migrations component
For non-trivial migrations use `@convex-dev/migrations` — it handles batching, cursor pagination, state tracking, resume-from-failure, dry runs, and progress monitoring. Run with `npx convex run migrations:myMigration` and test with `'{"dryRun": true}'`.

## Common pitfalls
Making a field required before migrating; using `.collect()` on large tables; not writing the new format before migrating; skipping the dry run; deleting fields prematurely.
skills.verified: Cloned github.com/get-convex/agent-skills; read skills/convex-migration-helper/SKILL.md

skills.name: convex-performance-audit
skills.description: Systematically diagnoses and fixes Convex performance issues by classifying them into read amplification, write contention, subscription cost, and function limits. Use to investigate Convex bottlenecks, OCC conflicts, slow UI, or function timeouts.
skills.author: Convex
skills.markdown_instructions:
# Convex Performance Audit

Diagnose and resolve performance issues by categorizing them into four classes: read amplification, write contention, subscription cost, and function limits.

## Workflow
1. **Scope** the problem to one concrete user flow.
2. **Trace** every read and write in that flow.
3. **Apply fixes** from the matching reference document.
4. **Fix sibling functions** consistently across the codebase.
5. **Verify** results with no regressions.

## Signal routing
- High bytes/documents read -> `references/hot-path-rules.md`
- OCC conflicts / write contention -> `references/occ-conflicts.md`
- High subscriptions / slow UI -> `references/subscription-cost.md`
- Timeouts / transaction limits -> `references/function-budget.md`

## Guardrails
Prefer simpler code when scale is small or signals are weak. Do not recommend heavy structural changes without measured performance data — always tie a recommended change back to a measured signal (bytes read, OCC retry count, subscription count, or function duration) rather than a guess.
skills.verified: Cloned github.com/get-convex/agent-skills; read skills/convex-performance-audit/SKILL.md

skills.name: clerk-nextjs-patterns
skills.description: Advanced Clerk authentication patterns for Next.js — server vs client auth APIs, middleware strategies, protecting Server Actions and API routes, user-scoped caching, custom JWTs via getToken(), and manual JWT verification.
skills.author: Clerk
skills.markdown_instructions:
# Clerk Next.js Patterns Advanced Next.js patterns — middleware, Server Actions, caching — with Clerk. For basic setup, see the `clerk-setup` skill. ## Mental model Server vs Client = different auth APIs: - **Server**: `await auth()` from `@clerk/nextjs/server` (async!) - **Client**: `useAuth()` hook from `@clerk/nextjs` (sync) Never mix them. Key properties from `auth()`: `isAuthenticated`, `sessionStatus`, `userId`, `orgId`, `has()`, `protect()`. ## Minimal pattern ```typescript import { auth } from '@clerk/nextjs/server' export default async function Page() { const { isAuthenticated, userId } = await auth() // MUST await! if (!isAuthenticated) return

Not signed in

return

Hello {userId}

} ``` ## Common pitfalls - `undefined` userId in Server Component -> missing `await`. - Auth not working on API routes -> add `'/(api|trpc)(.*)'` to the middleware matcher. - Cache returns wrong user's data -> include `userId` in the `unstable_cache` key. - Mutations bypass auth -> check `auth()` at the start of every Server Action. - 401 = not signed in, 403 = no permission. ## Custom JWTs & verification Use `getToken({ template: 'hasura' })` for third-party services (null-check it). For standalone API servers, verify tokens with `verifyToken(token, { jwtKey: process.env.CLERK_JWT_KEY })` from `@clerk/backend`, checking `exp` and `nbf`.
skills.verified: Cloned github.com/clerk/skills; read skills/frameworks/clerk-nextjs-patterns/SKILL.md (author: clerk, v2.2.0)

skills.name: auth0-nextjs-authentication
skills.description: Add authentication to Next.js apps with @auth0/nextjs-auth0 — login, logout, protected pages, middleware, and server components for both App Router and Pages Router.
skills.author: Auth0
skills.markdown_instructions:
# Auth0 Next.js Integration

Add authentication to Next.js applications using `@auth0/nextjs-auth0`. Supports both App Router and Pages Router.

## When NOT to use
- Client-side only React apps (Vite/CRA SPAs) -> use `auth0-react`.
- React Native -> use `auth0-react-native`.
- Stateless APIs only -> use JWT validation middleware.

## Quick start
1. Install: `npm install @auth0/nextjs-auth0`.
2. Configure `.env.local` (gitignored): `AUTH0_SECRET` (openssl rand -hex 32), `APP_BASE_URL`, `AUTH0_DOMAIN`, `AUTH0_CLIENT_ID`, `AUTH0_CLIENT_SECRET`.
3. Create the Auth0 client in `lib/auth0.ts`:
```typescript
import { Auth0Client } from '@auth0/nextjs-auth0/server';
export const auth0 = new Auth0Client({
  domain: process.env.AUTH0_DOMAIN!,
  clientId: process.env.AUTH0_CLIENT_ID!,
  clientSecret: process.env.AUTH0_CLIENT_SECRET!,
  secret: process.env.AUTH0_SECRET!,
  appBaseUrl: process.env.APP_BASE_URL!,
});
```
4. Wire up `middleware.ts` (Next.js 15) — or `proxy.ts` (Next.js 16) — to call the Auth0 client's middleware so login/logout/callback routes mount automatically. See `references/setup.md` for Auth0 CLI automation. Detect `src/` layout and place files accordingly.

skills.name: twilio-verify-otp
skills.description: Send and verify one-time passcodes via Twilio Verify over SMS, voice, email, WhatsApp, or RCS. Covers creating a Verify Service, sending and checking codes, channel fallback, and Fraud Guard.
skills.author: Twilio
skills.markdown_instructions:
# Twilio Verify — Send & Verify OTPs

Use Twilio Verify to manage the full OTP lifecycle: code generation, delivery, expiry, rate limiting, and Fraud Guard. Verify is A2P-exempt (no 10DLC registration), multi-channel via a single `channel` parameter, and bills per confirmed verification.

## Prerequisites
`TWILIO_ACCOUNT_SID`, `TWILIO_AUTH_TOKEN`, `VERIFY_SERVICE_SID`; SDK `pip install twilio` / `npm install twilio`.

## Quickstart
1. Create a Verify Service (one-time): `client.verify.v2.services.create(friendly_name="My App")` -> save the `VAxxxx` SID and reuse it.
2. Send a token: `client.verify.v2.services(SID).verifications.create(to="+1555...", channel="sms")` -> status `pending`.
3. Check the submitted code: `client.verify.v2.services(SID).verification_checks.create(to="+1555...", code="123456")` -> status `approved` or `pending`.

Change `channel` to `voice`, `email`, `whatsapp`, or `rcs` to switch delivery. Codes default to a 10-minute expiry (configurable). Verify enforces per-phone and per-service rate limiting automatically. Use the Programmable Messaging API only when you need full control of message content.

skills.name: sendgrid-email-send
skills.description: Send transactional and bulk email via the SendGrid v3 Mail Send API — single sends, personalized batch sends with dynamic templates, scheduled sends, attachments, and sandbox mode.
skills.author: Twilio SendGrid
skills.markdown_instructions:
# SendGrid Email Send Send transactional and bulk email through `POST /v3/mail/send`. > **Agent safety:** Always confirm recipients, subject, and content with the user before sending. Email is irreversible once delivered. Never send autonomously without explicit approval — especially batch sends. This endpoint returns `202 Accepted` (queued), NOT `200 OK`. Delivery confirmation arrives asynchronously via the Event Webhook. ## Basic send (Python) ```python import os, sendgrid from sendgrid.helpers.mail import Mail sg = sendgrid.SendGridAPIClient(os.environ["SENDGRID_API_KEY"]) message = Mail(from_email="verified@yourdomain.com", to_emails="recipient@example.com", subject="Order Confirmation", html_content="

Your order #1234 is confirmed.

") response = sg.send(message) # 202 = queued ``` ## Personalized batch send with dynamic templates Dynamic templates use Handlebars; template IDs start with `d-`. Pass per-recipient `dynamic_template_data` via `To(...)` objects and set `message.template_id`. The `from_email` must be a verified sender. Do NOT use this skill for the Twilio Email API (comms.twilio.com) — that's a separate product. Supports scheduled sends (with cancellation), attachments, and sandbox mode for testing.

skills.name: elevenlabs-text-to-speech
skills.description: Convert text to lifelike speech using ElevenLabs voice AI in 70+ languages — model selection for quality/latency tradeoffs, voice settings, language enforcement, streaming, request stitching, and output formats.
skills.author: ElevenLabs
skills.markdown_instructions:
# ElevenLabs Text-to-Speech

Generate natural speech from text — supports 70+ languages and multiple models for quality vs latency tradeoffs. Requires `ELEVENLABS_API_KEY`. For JavaScript, use `@elevenlabs/*` packages only.

## Quick start (Python)
```python
from elevenlabs import ElevenLabs
client = ElevenLabs()
audio = client.text_to_speech.convert(text="Hello!", voice_id="JBFqnCBsd6RMkjVDRZzb", model_id="eleven_multilingual_v2")
with open("output.mp3", "wb") as f:
    for chunk in audio: f.write(chunk)
```

## Models
- `eleven_v3` — 70+ languages, highest quality, emotional range.
- `eleven_multilingual_v2` — 29 languages, high quality, long-form.
- `eleven_flash_v2_5` — ~75ms latency, real-time.
- `eleven_turbo_v2_5` — ~250-300ms, balanced.

## Voice settings
Stability (lower = more emotion), similarity boost, style exaggeration, speaker boost. Use `language_code` (ISO 639-1) to force pronunciation, `apply_text_normalization` for numbers/dates, and `previous_text`/`next_text` request stitching to avoid pops across multi-request long audio.

## Streaming
Use `client.text_to_speech.stream(...)` and play chunks as they arrive. Output formats include `mp3_44100_128` (default), PCM for real-time, and `ulaw_8000`/`alaw_8000` for telephony. Common errors: 401 (bad key), 422 (invalid params), 429 (rate limit).

# ElevenLabs Sound Effects

Generate sound effects from text descriptions — supports looping, custom duration, and prompt adherence control. Requires `ELEVENLABS_API_KEY`. For JavaScript, use `@elevenlabs/*` packages only.

## Quick start (Python)
```python
from elevenlabs import ElevenLabs
client = ElevenLabs()
audio = client.text_to_sound_effects.convert(text="Thunder rumbling in the distance with light rain")
with open("thunder.mp3", "wb") as f:
    for chunk in audio: f.write(chunk)
```

## cURL
```bash
curl -X POST "https://api.elevenlabs.io/v1/sound-generation" \
  -H "xi-api-key: $ELEVENLABS_API_KEY" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"text": "Thunder rumbling in the distance with light rain"}' --output thunder.mp3
```

Write descriptive prompts (the instrument/source, the action, and the environment) for best results. Parameters let you control output duration, enable seamless looping for ambiences and UI loops, and tune prompt influence to trade fidelity to the prompt against naturalness. Good for cinematic impacts, ambient beds, and short interface sounds.

skills.name: shopify-liquid-themes
skills.description: Build Shopify theme components in Liquid — sections, blocks, and snippets following Shopify theme architecture and schema conventions, with docs search and code validation.
skills.author: Shopify
skills.markdown_instructions:
# Shopify Liquid Theme Development

Liquid is Shopify's open-source templating language and the backbone of Shopify themes. This skill generates theme components consistent with Shopify's architecture.

## Required workflow (do not skip)
Every response must use the bundled scripts via `bash`, in order:
1. `scripts/search_docs.mjs "<query>"` — search docs before writing code.
2. Write the code using the search results.
3. `scripts/validate.mjs` on the generated code before returning it.
4. If validation fails, search the error type, fix, and re-validate (max 3 retries).
5. Return code only after validation passes.

## Theme architecture
Focus on generating snippets, blocks, and sections; merchants create templates via the theme editor. Directory layout: `assets`, `blocks`, `config`, `layout`, `locales`, `sections`, `snippets`, `templates`.
- **Sections**: `.liquid` modules customizable by merchants; must include a `{% schema %}` tag; use `{{ block.shopify_attributes }}` on block wrappers for theme-editor drag-and-drop.
- **Blocks**: reusable, nestable components.
- **Snippets**: reusable fragments included via `{% render %}`.

Help developers understand, design, and generate Admin GraphQL operations for Shopify apps and integrations.

2. Write valid GraphQL based on the search results.
3. Validate with `scripts/validate.mjs` before returning. On failure, search the error type, fix, and re-validate (max 3 retries).
4. Explain what the operation does, with documentation links.

The skill enforces the search-and-validate loop so generated GraphQL matches the current Admin API schema rather than memorized field names.

skills.markdown_instructions:
# Datadog Integration Skill

Interact with Datadog two ways: **immediate execution** with the `pup` CLI, and **code generation** for application integration using Datadog API clients.

## When to use
Querying Datadog data (logs, traces, metrics, events, RUM), configuring Datadog (monitors, dashboards, SLOs, synthetics), or generating code to integrate Datadog into an application.

## Pup CLI
A Go wrapper for Datadog APIs with OAuth2 (`pup auth login`) or API-key auth (`DD_API_KEY`, `DD_APP_KEY`, `DD_SITE`):
```bash
pup metrics query --query="avg:system.cpu.user{*}" --from="1h" --to="now"
pup logs search --query="status:error service:api" --from="30m"
pup monitors list --tag="team:backend"
```

## Code generation
Clients exist for TypeScript (`@datadog/datadog-api-client`), Python (`datadog-api-client`), Java, Go, and Rust.

## When to use each
- **Pup CLI**: immediate results, exploration, one-off queries, quick troubleshooting.
- **Code generation**: integrating into an app, automating recurring operations, when the user names a language.

## Best practices
Start with pup to test queries before generating code; match the user's language; provide complete examples (imports, error handling, config); explain `DD_API_KEY`/`DD_APP_KEY`/`DD_SITE` auth; warn against committing credentials.

skills.name: browserbase-browser-automation
skills.description: Automate web browser interactions via the browse CLI — navigate, snapshot, extract data, screenshot, fill forms, and click, in local Chrome or remote Browserbase sessions with CAPTCHA solving and residential proxies.
skills.author: Browserbase
skills.markdown_instructions:
# Browser Automation

Automate browser interactions using the `browse` CLI. Before running any command, verify it's installed: `which browse || npm install -g browse`.

## Environment selection (local vs remote)
The next session defaults to Browserbase when `BROWSERBASE_API_KEY` is set, otherwise local.
- **Local:** `browse open <url> --local` (clean isolated browser), `--auto-connect` (attach to running Chrome, reuse login/cookies), `--cdp <port|url>` (specific target). Best for development, localhost, trusted sites.
- **Remote (Browserbase):** `browse open <url> --remote`. Provides verified browsers, automatic CAPTCHA solving, residential proxies, and session persistence. Use when the site has bot detection, CAPTCHAs, IP rate limiting, or Cloudflare protection.

## Choosing
Repeatable local testing -> `--local`; reuse your login -> `--auto-connect`; simple browsing -> local; protected sites -> remote. If local fails with bot detection, switch to remote.

## Commands
```bash
browse open <url>      # navigate
browse reload          # reload
browse back / forward  # history
```
Prefer `browse snapshot` (accessibility tree with element references) over screenshots for inspecting and interacting with pages. Run `browse stop` to clean up when finished.
skills.verified: Cloned github.com/browserbase/skills; read skills/browser/SKILL.md (author: browserbase, MIT). Repo has 14 skills.

skills.name: browserbase-ui-test
skills.description: AI-powered adversarial UI testing via the browse CLI — analyzes a git diff to test only what changed or explores the full app to find bugs, covering functional correctness, accessibility, responsive layout, and UX.
skills.author: Browserbase
skills.markdown_instructions:
# UI Test — Agentic UI Testing

Test UI changes in a real browser. Your job is to **try to break things**, not confirm they work. Three workflows: diff-driven (test only what changed), exploratory (find unanticipated bugs), and parallel (fan out across multiple Browserbase browsers).

## How testing works
The main agent coordinates — plans, delegates to sub-agents, and merges results. Sub-agents do the actual browser testing.

## Planning: three rounds, then execute once
Complete all three planning rounds before launching sub-agents:
- **Round 1 (Functional):** core user flows as action -> expected result.
- **Round 2 (Adversarial):** error paths, empty states, race conditions, edge inputs (empty, huge, special chars, rapid clicks).
- **Round 3 (Coverage gaps):** accessibility (axe-core, keyboard-only), mobile viewports, console errors, visual consistency.
Deduplicate into one numbered list, assign each test to a group, then launch one sub-agent per group.

## Step budgets
Every sub-agent prompt MUST include an explicit browse step limit (~25 small, ~40 full page, ~75 multi-page). When a sub-agent hits its budget it reports STEP_PASS/STEP_FAIL per completed test and STEP_SKIP for the rest, then stops — no retries. The main agent merges results into a single report with no early stopping on failures.
skills.verified: Cloned github.com/browserbase/skills; read skills/ui-test/SKILL.md (author: browserbase, v0.4.0, MIT)

skills.name: d3-viz
skills.description: Build bespoke, interactive data visualizations with d3.js — custom charts, network/force-directed graphs, chord diagrams, heatmaps, geographic maps, and choreographed transitions — in vanilla JS, React, Vue, or Svelte.
skills.author: Chris von Csefalvay
skills.markdown_instructions:
# D3.js Visualisation

Use when creating custom charts, graphs, network diagrams, geographic visualisations, or any complex SVG-based visualisation requiring fine-grained control over visual elements, transitions, or interactions — beyond standard charting libraries. Works in vanilla JS, React, Vue, Svelte.

## When to use d3.js
Use for unique visual encodings, interactive pan/zoom/brush, network/graph layouts (force-directed, trees, chord), geographic projections, smooth transitions, and novel chart types. For 3D, use Three.js instead.

## Core workflow
1. Import: `import * as d3 from 'd3'` or CDN.
2. Choose a pattern: Pattern A (direct DOM manipulation — recommended for complex viz) or Pattern B (d3 for scale/layout math, framework for rendering).
3. Structure the drawing function: dimensions/margins, a main `g` group, scales (y-range inverted for SVG coords), axes, then bind data with `.data().join()`.
4. Make it responsive with a resize listener or `ResizeObserver`.

## Patterns provided
Bar, line, scatter, pie, chord diagram, heatmap with color legend, and force-directed network with drag handlers.

## Best practices
Validate/clean data (filter NaN, parse dates), use canvas for >1000 elements, add ARIA labels/title/desc, and use `.join()` over manual enter/update/exit. Reference files: `d3-patterns.md`, `scale-reference.md`, `colour-schemes.md`.

skills.name: ffuf-web-fuzzing
skills.description: Expert guidance for ffuf web fuzzing during AUTHORIZED penetration testing: directory/file discovery, subdomain enumeration, parameter and POST/header fuzzing, authenticated raw-request fuzzing, auto-calibration, and result analysis.
skills.author: Jason Haddix (jthack)
trigger_phrases: fuzz this web app with ffuf
skills.markdown_instructions:
# ffuf (Fuzz Faster U Fool)

Expert guidance for ffuf web fuzzing during authorized penetration testing. ffuf is a fast Go web fuzzer for discovering hidden content, directories, files, subdomains, and testing for vulnerabilities. Use only against targets you are authorized to test.

## Installation
`go install github.com/ffuf/ffuf/v2@latest`, `brew install ffuf`, or a release binary.

## The FUZZ keyword
A placeholder substituted with wordlist entries in URLs, headers, POST data, and custom positions. Multi-wordlist modes: clusterbomb (cartesian product), pitchfork (parallel 1:1), sniper (single position).

## Primary use cases
Directory/file discovery (`-e` extensions, recursion), subdomain enumeration (vhost with size filtering), GET/POST parameter discovery, JSON/multi-field POST fuzzing, and header fuzzing.

## Matchers & filters
Inclusion (matchers) and exclusion (filters) on status codes, line/word counts, response size, regex, and timing.

## Auto-calibration (critical)
Use `-ac` by DEFAULT on every scan — it auto-detects false positives and adapts to target behavior.

## Raw HTTP requests (most powerful)
Capture an authenticated request to a file, replace the target value with `FUZZ`, then `ffuf --request req.txt -w wordlist.txt -ac`. This handles JWTs, CSRF tokens, session cookies, and IDOR testing. Use `-rate`, random delays `-p 0.1-2.0`, and threads `-t` responsibly. Output to JSON/HTML/CSV; analyze results by anomalies, not volume.
skills.verified: Fetched raw SKILL.md from jthack/ffuf_claude_skill (name: ffuf-web-fuzzing)

# Playwright Skill

Run `cd $SKILL_DIR && npm run setup` to install Playwright and Chromium.

1. **Server detection** — automatically identify running dev servers before testing localhost.
2. **Script generation** — write custom Playwright scripts into `/tmp` rather than cluttering the project directory.

- Parameterize URLs at the top of each script using a `TARGET_URL` constant.
- Wrap actions in try-catch blocks for proper error handling.

## Common use cases
Responsive design testing across viewport sizes, login flow validation, form submission, broken link detection, and screenshot capture.

skills.name: frontend-slides
skills.description: Create animation-rich, zero-dependency HTML presentations from scratch or by converting PowerPoint files. Uses a fixed 16:9 stage, distinctive (non-templated) design, and a visual style-discovery workflow.
skills.author: Zara Zhang (zarazhangrui)
skills.markdown_instructions:
# Frontend Slides

Create stunning, animation-rich HTML presentations from scratch or by converting PowerPoint files. Use when the user wants to build a presentation, convert a PPT/PPTX to web, or create slides for a talk/pitch. Generates zero-dependency, single-file HTML decks that run entirely in the browser.

## Core principles
- **Zero dependencies** — single HTML file with inline CSS/JS.
- **Show, don't tell** — generate visual previews so users discover design preferences visually.
- **Distinctive design** — avoid 'AI slop': no overused fonts (Inter, Roboto), no purple gradients, no predictable layouts.
- **Fixed 16:9 stage (NON-NEGOTIABLE)** — every deck uses a 1920x1080 canvas scaled uniformly to the viewport.

## Six-phase workflow
- Phase 0: Detect mode (new / PPT conversion / enhance existing).
- Phase 1: Content discovery — ask Purpose, Length, Content-readiness, Density.
- Phase 2: Style discovery — generate 3 single-slide previews (safe, bold, wildcard); user picks.
- Phase 3: Generate the full deck as one self-contained HTML file with `viewport-base.css` inlined.
- Phase 4: PPT conversion — extract .pptx content, confirm, then generate HTML preserving text/images/order.
- Phase 5: Delivery — launch in browser, explain navigation and CSS-variable customization.
- Phase 6: Share/export — Vercel deploy or Playwright PDF export.

Control slide visibility with `.active`/`.visible` (never `display:none`); include `prefers-reduced-motion`; use Fontshare/Google Fonts, never system fonts.

# iOS Simulator Skill

Prioritize semantic interaction over visual analysis: ALWAYS prefer the accessibility tree over screenshots — structured element data needs ~10-50 tokens vs 1,600-6,300 for screenshot analysis. Three-tier workflow:

skills.name: mermaid-diagram
skills.description: Create Mermaid diagram files that make a visual argument — flowcharts, sequence, state, ER, mindmap, timeline, and C4 diagrams. Includes a mandatory render-and-validate loop to avoid syntax errors.
skills.author: Matthew Granberry (mgranberry)
skills.markdown_instructions:
# Mermaid Diagram

Create Mermaid diagram files that make visual arguments. Use when the user wants to visualize workflows, architectures, or concepts.

## Core philosophy
Diagrams should ARGUE, not DISPLAY. Communicate relationships and causality through visual structure itself. Apply the Isomorphism Test: removing all text should leave a structure that still conveys the concept.

## Design process
- Step 0: Assess simple/conceptual vs comprehensive/technical depth.
- Step 1: Research actual specs, formats, and real terminology — no generic placeholders.
- Steps 2-5: Map concepts to visual patterns (fan-out, convergence, hierarchy, timelines, cycles, comparisons, phases).
- Step 6: Generate Mermaid syntax using a Decision Matrix and apply semantic styling via `classDef`.
- Step 7: Validate by running the render script and iteratively improving layout.

## Decision matrix
Fan-out/convergence -> flowchart; sequences -> sequence diagram; hierarchies -> mindmap; state machines -> state diagram; system architecture -> C4; timelines -> timeline; entity relationships -> ER. Excluded: pie, gantt, git graph, XY charts, user journey.

## Render & validate (mandatory)
Run `bash render_mermaid.sh <path.mmd>` to validate syntax. Always quote labels containing parentheses, commas, `@`, `/`, or special chars: `["label"]`. Use modern `flowchart TD` (never legacy `graph TD`). Minimize line crossings via declaration order, `rankDir`, and subgraph grouping.

Analyzes CSV files, generates summary stats, and plots quick visualizations using Python and pandas. Dependencies: python>=3.8, pandas>=2.0.0, matplotlib>=3.7.0, seaborn>=0.12.0.

## Core directive
Immediately execute comprehensive analysis WITHOUT soliciting user input. Run the full analysis, generate ALL relevant visualizations, and present complete results — no questions, no options, no waiting for user input.

## Automatic behavior
Examine the uploaded data structure and adapt to detected column types:
- Time-series analysis only when date columns exist.
- Correlation matrices when multiple numeric fields are present.
- Categorical distributions for categorical data.

## Prohibited actions
Never ask 'What would you like to do with this data?' or present a list of possible analyses. Avoid conditional language ('I can do X if you want').

## Output approach
Deliver the complete analysis in a single response: dataset overview, statistical summary, missing-data assessment, relevant visualizations, and data-specific insights. The `summarize_csv(file_path)` function returns statistical summaries plus auto-generated visualizations, adapting across domains based on the actual data.

skills.name: postgres-readonly-query
skills.description: Execute safe, read-only SQL queries against one or more PostgreSQL databases — explore schemas/tables, run SELECTs for analysis, and check contents. Blocks all write operations (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/DROP) and supports multi-database auto-selection.
skills.author: sanjay3290
skills.markdown_instructions:
# Postgres (read-only)

Execute read-only SQL queries against multiple PostgreSQL databases. Use when querying databases, exploring schemas/tables, running SELECTs for analysis, or checking contents. Supports multiple connections with descriptions for intelligent auto-selection. Blocks all write operations for safety. License: Apache-2.0.

## Setup
Requires Python 3.8+ and `psycopg2-binary`. Create a `connections.json` with database credentials and set its permissions to `600`. Required fields per connection: name, description, host, database, user, password.

## Operations
- List databases: `--list`
- Execute queries: `--query "SELECT ..."`
- Explore structure: `--tables` or `--schema`
- Apply row limits: `--limit`

## Safety mechanisms
- Only SELECT, SHOW, EXPLAIN, and WITH queries are allowed.
- Single statement only — multiple statements per query are rejected.
- 30-second query timeout and a cap of 10,000 rows.

Troubleshooting guidance covers missing configs, authentication failures, connection timeouts, and SSL errors, with exit codes indicating success or failure type.

skills.name: email-html-mjml
skills.description: Generate cross-client responsive HTML email templates using MJML 4.x — welcome emails, promotional blasts, transactional templates, and newsletters. Handles Outlook/Gmail/iOS quirks, dark mode, accessibility, and compiles to production HTML.
skills.author: framix-team
skills.markdown_instructions:
# email-html-mjml

Generate cross-client responsive email templates using the MJML 4.x framework. Use when the user needs to create, design, or build an email template — welcome emails, promotional blasts, transactional templates, and newsletters. MIT-licensed; requires Node.js 14+ and per-project `npm install -D mjml`.

## Workflow
1. Gather requirements from context, asking only for genuinely missing information.
2. Plan the layout and announce the structure before coding.
3. Load component references from the provided index.
4. Generate MJML from the root `<mjml>` tag with a complete `<mj-head>`.
5. Compile and deliver both `.mjml` source and `.html` output.

## Engineering rules
- All visual content lives in `<mj-column>` within `<mj-section>` (sections cannot nest).
- Assume 600px width; use `<mj-group>` to prevent unwanted mobile stacking.
- Outlook: use `<mj-font>` with fallback stacks; background images need both `background-size` and a fallback color.
- Gmail: prefer component attributes over CSS classes; minify to respect the ~102KB clip limit.
- Dark mode: use safe neutrals like `#121212`, not pure black.
- Accessibility: every image needs `alt`; set `<mj-title>`; maintain 4.5:1 contrast.
- Wrap dynamic Handlebars/Liquid tags in `<mj-raw>`. JS is completely blocked in all email clients.

## Compilation
`npx mjml template.mjml -o dist/template.html --config.minify=true --config.validationLevel=strict` (never global install). Always deliver both the editable `.mjml` and the compiled `.html`.

skills.name: aws-architecture-diagram
skills.description: Generate editable AWS architecture diagrams as native .drawio files using official AWS Architecture Icons (mxgraph.aws4 stencils), with verified icon names, left-to-right data flow layouts, and PNG/SVG/PDF export.
skills.author: Andrei Vidanov (vidanov)
skills.markdown_instructions:
# AWS Architecture Diagram

Generate AWS architecture diagrams as native `.drawio` files using official AWS Architecture Icons from draw.io's `mxgraph.aws4` stencil library.

## Diagram generation
Create left-to-right data-flow layouts: UI/frontend on the left, data sources on the right. Support horizontal lanes for parallel paths and secondary services below the main flow. Canvas 2400x1400px.

## Icon standards
Main services 78x78px, secondary 65x65px. All AWS icons use `sketch=0` and `strokeColor=#ffffff`, 12px label font, and service-specific fill colors (Lambda `#ED7100`, DynamoDB `#C925D1`).

## Edge/connection rules
Orthogonal edges, 2px stroke. Edge labels 1-2 words max. Solid lines for primary flow, dashed for optional/async, red dashed for errors. Every edge needs explicit valid `source`/`target` cell IDs.

## Critical requirements
- Container nesting: group boundaries need `container=1;dropTarget=1;` and child cells set `parent="<boundary-id>"`.
- Icon verification: empty boxes mean an incorrect stencil name (e.g. 'MSK' maps to `managed_streaming_for_kafka`).
- XML well-formedness: no XML comments; escape `&`, `<`, `>`, `"`.

## Export
PNG/SVG/PDF via the draw.io CLI with embedded XML for re-editing. Technical audiences get service names and protocol labels; non-technical audiences get action-oriented labels with numbered flow indicators.

skills.name: elevenlabs-tts-podcast
skills.description: Convert documents and text into audio using the ElevenLabs TTS API — single-voice narration of PDF/DOCX/markdown, and two-host podcast generation from a JSON script with automatic chunking and ffmpeg concatenation.
skills.author: sanjay3290
trigger_phrases: create a podcast from this document
skills.markdown_instructions:
# ElevenLabs TTS & Podcast

Convert documents and text into audio using ElevenLabs' TTS API, supporting both single-voice narration and two-host podcast generation. Activates for requests like 'create podcast', 'narrate document', or 'text to speech'. Requires an ElevenLabs API key and ffmpeg for multi-chunk processing.

## Voice management
List available voices with the `voices` command to help the user select. Suggest contrasting voice pairs for podcasts.

## Single-voice narration
Convert text or documents (PDF, DOCX, markdown) to audio. The system handles text extraction, chunking at sentence boundaries (~4000 chars), TTS per chunk with voice continuity, and ffmpeg concatenation automatically.

## Podcast generation
Create a conversation from a JSON script with alternating speakers. Workflow: extract document text -> generate a natural two-host discussion script -> produce the final audio file. Write the script as a natural, engaging discussion rather than verbatim reading; differentiate host roles (one introduces topics, the other provides analysis).

## Practical guidance
Run the voice command first, default outputs to Downloads, and warn the user about character limits for large documents.

skills.name: code-review-multilang
skills.description: Comprehensive code-review guidance across 20+ languages and frameworks (React 19, Vue 3, Rust, TypeScript, Go, Python, Java, Swift, Kotlin, and more). Uses a four-phase process and severity labels to catch bugs and give constructive feedback.
skills.author: awesome-skills
skills.markdown_instructions:
# Code Review Skill

Comprehensive code review guidance for React 19, Vue 3, Angular 17+, Svelte 5, Rust, TypeScript, Java, PHP, Python, Django, Go, C#/.NET, Kotlin, Swift, NestJS, C/C++, and more. Use when reviewing pull requests, establishing review standards, mentoring developers, or conducting architecture/security reviews. Goal: transform code reviews from gatekeeping into knowledge sharing.

## Core principles
Goals: catch bugs and edge cases, ensure maintainability, share knowledge, enforce standards, improve design — NOT to nitpick formatting (use linters), block progress, or rewrite to personal preference. Feedback should be specific, kind, and actionable.

## Four-phase review process
1. **Context gathering** — understand PR scope and intent.
2. **High-level review** — architecture, performance, testing strategy.
3. **Line-by-line analysis** — logic, security, maintainability, edge cases.
4. **Summary & decision** — structured feedback and approval status.

## Severity labels
Use `blocking`, `important`, `nit`, `suggestion`, `learning`, and `praise` to standardize feedback while keeping a collaborative tone.

## Progressive disclosure
The core skill stays minimal (~190 lines); detailed language-specific guides load on demand for the relevant stack. Allowed tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash (lint/test/build), WebFetch (current docs).

skills.name: github-pr-review
skills.description: Review GitHub pull requests with the gh CLI — creates pending reviews with batched code-suggestion comments, chooses the right event type (COMMENT/APPROVE/REQUEST_CHANGES), and requires explicit user approval before posting anything.
skills.author: Aidan Kinzett (aidankinzett)
trigger_phrases: add code suggestions to the PR
skills.markdown_instructions:
# GitHub PR Review

Use when reviewing GitHub pull requests with the gh CLI — creates pending reviews with code suggestions, batches comments, and chooses appropriate event types (COMMENT/APPROVE/REQUEST_CHANGES). Always use pending reviews to batch comments.

**CRITICAL: Always get explicit user approval before posting any review comments.** Show exactly what will be posted and ask for yes/no confirmation.

## Prerequisites
Check that the gh CLI is installed before starting: `gh --version`. If gh is not installed, stop immediately, do not run any `gh api` commands, and inform the user that the GitHub CLI is required.

## Workflow (three stages)
1. **Draft review** — analyze the PR and prepare comments with code suggestions.
2. **Show & get approval** — display exactly what will be posted (file locations, suggestions, event type) and ask for confirmation.
3. **Post review** — after explicit approval, submit the pending review via `gh api`.

## Key rules
Batch comments in a single pending review; use code-suggestion blocks with proper syntax; select the appropriate event type; apply correct gh CLI syntax with proper quoting.

skills.name: career-ops
skills.description: An AI job-search command center: evaluate job offers, generate ATS-friendly CVs/cover letters (PDF and LaTeX), scan job portals, track applications, and run interview prep. Routes by mode and can auto-pipeline from a pasted job description or URL.
skills.author: santifer
trigger_phrases: tailor my resume for this JD
skills.markdown_instructions:
# career-ops — Router

AI job search command center — evaluate offers, generate CVs, scan portals, track applications. License: MIT. User-invocable with a `mode` argument.

## Mode routing
Determine the mode from `$mode`:
- (empty / no args) -> `discovery` (show the command menu).
- JD text or URL with no sub-command -> `auto-pipeline`.
- Known sub-commands include: `oferta`, `ofertas`, `contacto`, `deep`, `interview-prep`, `interview`, `pdf`, `latex`, `training`, `project`, `tracker`, `pipeline`, `apply`, `scan`, `batch`, `patterns`, `followup`, `update`, `cover`.

## Auto-pipeline detection
If `$mode` is not a known sub-command AND contains JD text (keywords: 'responsibilities', 'requirements', 'qualifications', 'about the role', company name + role) or a URL to a JD, execute `auto-pipeline`. Otherwise show discovery.

## Capabilities
JD evaluation, ATS-friendly resume/CV generation (PDF and LaTeX output), cover-letter writing, portal/offer scanning, application tracking and pipeline management, contact management, interview preparation and pattern analysis, and follow-up handling — routed through the modes above.
